spec_match.rationale: Three scenarios each invoke a real PaymentRequest via the same-origin handler and parse the structured JSON envelope out of `OperationError.message` — exactly the contract the feature introduces.

interactive.rationale: Scenario tabs, live form, real Pay button, install/canMakePayment controls, field-error highlighting, and a recovery-step list driven by JS — solid hands-on demo.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises field highlighting, retry guidance, and graceful fallback per error type — all three are rendered as the scenarios run.

edge_cases.rationale: Three OperationError variants are covered well, but there is no AbortError variant (every scenario triggers an error) and no demonstration of what happens when `err.message` is NOT valid JSON — the catch block's `try { JSON.parse }` swallows it silently.

design_system.rationale: Variables only, no raw hex, button colour is overridden together with background. But the recovery-step list uses literal numeric labels (`1.`, `2.`) where the design system would expect either inline SVG markers or styled numerals; minor but worth noting.

openQuestions.title: Add an AbortError scenario tab
openQuestions.detail: All three scenarios in the lab are OperationError variants. The whole reason the spec change matters is that AbortError now means something specific (user intent). Add a fourth tab `user cancels` that resolves with AbortError and shows the merchant doing nothing (no retry, no warning) — contrast is the lesson.

openQuestions.title: Show what happens when err.message isn't JSON
openQuestions.detail: Production handlers may emit a plain string message. The catch block currently swallows the JSON.parse failure into an empty object, then the switch falls through to no-op. Add a `non-JSON message` toggle that surfaces the raw err.message and the fallback path — otherwise the demo encourages an unsafe pattern.

openQuestions.title: Highlight that the JSON envelope is convention, not spec
openQuestions.detail: Nothing in the spec mandates JSON in `OperationError.message` — the handler picked that envelope. The page reads as if the structure is standard. Add a short note disclaiming this, ideally next to the code block, so the reader knows the parsing pattern is theirs to design.

openQuestions.title: Demonstrate the retry-after-ms wait loop
openQuestions.detail: The timeout scenario sets `retry_after_ms: 1000` but the lab fires a single retry without a visible wait. Animate the retry: show a countdown / spinner using the value from the JSON envelope so the visitor sees the back-off policy in action.

openQuestions.title: Wire the field-error mapping to a real field map
openQuestions.detail: Card-expired highlights the expiry field, but the map is implicit in JS. Surface a tiny `field-to-input` table or comment showing how the handler's `field: "expiry"` becomes `#expiry.field-error` — that's the load-bearing pattern, currently buried.

summary: A genuinely strong recovery lab — real handler, real form, three meaningful OperationError variants — let down only by the lack of an AbortError contrast and an implicit reliance on a JSON-shaped err.message that the spec doesn't require.
